fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the core execution engine primitives for the blockchain node project. This is similar to what we did with the value encoding layer on the wallet service — same idea, just applied here to block/tx domain objects. The main ask is that we have a clean way to handle all the different 'block selector' strings that RPC clients throw at us (you know how messy those can get), plus we need solid conversion logic for those database decimal strings into the actual integer types the engine uses internally. There's also the block header derivation piece — we need deterministic hashes with proper parent linkage, and genesis should handle the zero-case correctly. On top of that we need to be able to ingest external block and receipt documents and decode them into our internal model. Finally there's a transaction execution path that runs against some pre-seeded in-memory state — the gas reporting needs to correctly distinguish between a regular transfer and the creation case. One thing that keeps biting us: errors surfaced to the output layer need to be clean category strings, not raw exception dumps. The output format is the flat key=value style we agreed on. Please make sure the code is properly split across files — last time someone just dumped everything in one file and it was a nightmare to review.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for block headers, the hash should be a deterministic 32-byte digest derived solely from the block height, so the timestamp does NOT affect it. Parent linkage is just based on height too, meaning the parent hash for height N is simply the derived hash of height N-1. For genesis at height 0, use the all-zero 32-byte hash as the parent hash, rendered as 0x0000...0000, 64 hex zeros. In general, hashes in output should be rendered as 0x-prefixed 64-hex-digit strings.

On the block selector side, keyword matching is case-insensitive for latest, pending, earliest, and 'pending' is just an alias for 'latest'. There is no separate pending state here, so both should resolve the same way and output selection=latest. Also, if the selector is a hex string that is exactly 64 hex digits long without a prefix, OR exactly 66 characters including the '0x' prefix, treat it as a hash selection and echo the canonical hash. Any other hex-prefixed value should be treated as a numeric height selection and should echo the decimal height. If it’s not a recognized keyword, valid hash, or valid hex number, return a neutral error and echo the raw selector. This matches the Block Selector Parsing section of the PRD and what’s defined in feature2_block_selector.json.

For execution, the state should be a freshly initialized in-memory state seeded with funded well-known accounts indexed by integer position. Gas is the simple split we discussed: a plain value transfer between two accounts where 'to_account' is provided uses 21000 gas, and a transaction with no recipient field, treated as an empty contract creation where only 'from_account' is provided, uses 53000 gas.

Also want to be extra explicit on output hygiene: every error surfaced to stdout MUST be normalized to a neutral category line in the form error=<category>, with any offending parameter on its own field line like selector=<raw>, field=<name>, or value=<raw>. We should not leak host-language exception class names, runtime message suffixes, object-repr renderings, stack traces, or internal type names.

Last thing, for feature 7 in the PRD, encode_value needs round-trip handling for address (20-byte, 0x-prefixed 40-hex-digit), hash (32-byte, 0x-prefixed 64-hex-digit), bytes (0x-prefixed hex), block_number (minimal hex, no leading zeros), gas (minimal hex), and execution_result (bare string or raw JSON object). Those are the cases covered across feature7_1 through feature7_6 test case files.

trigger_keywords: what happens if a decimal value has a fractional part
can you convert a non-integer decimal to wei
what error is raised for fractional scaled decimal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A decimal value that is not a whole number (it has a fractional remainder after scale expansion) cannot be represented as an integer amount and yields a neutral out-of-range error echoing the raw value. A positive power-of-ten scale (e.g. '1E+4') is expanded to its full integer form ('10000'). Plain integer strings pass through unchanged. This applies to both 'wei' and 'nonce' kinds.

trigger_keywords: how should receipts be ordered in output
what order are transaction hashes listed
sorting order for decoded receipt hashes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After decoding a batch of receipts, the output reports the number of distinct transactions indexed, followed by one line per indexed transaction hash. Hashes are listed in ascending lexicographic order (0x-prefixed 64-hex-digit strings sorted ascending). Format: 'transaction_count=N' then one 'transaction_hash=0x...' line per hash.

trigger_keywords: how should execution_result halted be encoded
what does the encoded output look like for a halted result
format for halted execution result vs success
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A normal completion is rendered as the bare string 'success'; a reversion is rendered as 'reverted'. An incomplete execution (halted) is rendered as a tagged JSON object carrying a free-text reason, e.g.: encoded={"halted":{"reason":"OutOfGas"}}. Note that the encoded value for success and reverted is quoted (e.g. encoded="success") but the halted object is NOT quoted — it is a raw JSON object literal in the output line.

trigger_keywords: what is the file structure expected
how should the code be organized across files
monolithic file vs multi-file layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase MUST NOT be a single god file. It must be a multi-file directory tree with a src/ directory for the core domain split by concern (value primitives, hashing/derivation, parsers, VM execution, storage), plus a separate adapter entry point and a tests/ directory. The JSON adapter is solely responsible for translating JSON commands into core domain calls and must remain decoupled from the core system.

trigger_keywords: how is block number reported in decode_block output
what format is the block height in decode_block response
decimal vs hex for block number in decoded block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The decoded block height is reported as a decimal integer in the output (e.g., 'number=58463146'), not as a hex string, even though the raw JSON block document carries it as a 0x-prefixed hex string. The transaction count is also reported as a plain decimal integer (e.g., 'transaction_count=1').

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The in-memory state for transaction execution (Feature 6) is initialized with funded well-known accounts accessible by integer index (from_account and to_account fields are integer indices into this seeded account set, not raw addresses).

context_pointers.vague_reference: the flat key=value style we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The adapter output format: a flat newline-terminated key=value record where every line including the last ends with a newline character. Defined in the 'Adapter command convention' section of the PRD.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from the original discussions. Please refer to the PRD (start.md) for the authoritative specification, and check the public test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output contract. If something is still unclear, make a reasonable design choice consistent with the patterns already established in the other features.

feature1_block_header_derivation.json.description: Derive a block header from a block height and a timestamp. The output reports the height, the deterministic block hash computed from the height, and the parent hash (the hash of the previous height, or the all-zero hash for the genesis block at height 0).

feature2_block_selector.json.description: Parse a block selector string into a tagged selection. The keywords 'latest' and 'pending' both resolve to the latest selection; 'earliest' resolves to the earliest selection; a 64- or 66-character hex string resolves to a hash selection; any other hex value resolves to a numeric height selection. Unparseable input yields a neutral error.

feature3_1_decimal_to_wei.json.description: Convert a scaled decimal quantity into an unsigned integer native-token amount. A decimal expressed with a positive power-of-ten scale is expanded to its full integer form. A fractional value that is not a whole number cannot be represented and yields a neutral out-of-range error.

feature3_2_decimal_to_nonce.json.description: Convert a scaled decimal quantity into an unsigned integer transaction sequence number. A decimal expressed with a positive power-of-ten scale is expanded to its full integer form.

feature4_block_decoding.json.description: Decode a JSON-encoded block document and report its height (as a decimal integer) and the number of transactions it contains.

feature5_receipt_decoding.json.description: Decode a list of JSON-encoded transaction receipts into a set keyed by transaction hash, then report how many distinct transactions were indexed and list their transaction hashes.

feature6_transaction_execution.json.description: Execute a zero-value transfer through the virtual machine against a freshly initialized in-memory state seeded with funded well-known accounts. The sender and recipient are chosen by their index in the seeded account set. The output reports the execution result category and the gas consumed: a plain value transfer between two accounts consumes the base transfer cost, while a transaction with no recipient (treated as an empty contract creation) consumes the base creation cost.

feature7_1_roundtrip_address.json.description: Decode an account address from its lowercase hex string wire form and re-emit the canonical encoding. A 20-byte address is rendered as a 0x-prefixed 40-hex-digit string. Malformed hex yields a neutral invalid-value error.

feature7_2_roundtrip_hash.json.description: Decode a 32-byte hash from its 0x-prefixed hex string wire form and re-emit the canonical 0x-prefixed 64-hex-digit encoding.

feature7_3_roundtrip_bytes.json.description: Decode an arbitrary byte payload from its 0x-prefixed hex string wire form and re-emit the canonical 0x-prefixed hex encoding.

feature7_4_roundtrip_block_number.json.description: Decode a block height from its 0x-prefixed minimal hex wire form and re-emit the canonical minimal hex encoding (no leading-zero padding).

feature7_5_roundtrip_gas.json.description: Decode a gas quantity from its 0x-prefixed hex wire form and re-emit the canonical minimal hex encoding.

feature7_6_roundtrip_execution_result.js.description: Decode an execution-outcome tag from its wire form and re-emit the canonical encoding. A normal completion and a reversion are rendered as the bare strings 'success' and 'reverted'; an incomplete execution is rendered as a tagged object carrying a free-text reason.
